X

Towards the end of June there were frequent excursions.

Into all the gatherings at WaldstraГҹe the outside world came like a presence. It removed the sense of pressure, of being confronted and challenged. Everything that was said seemed to be incidental to it, like remarks dropped in a low tone between individuals at a great conference.

Miriam wondered again and again whether her companions shared this sense with her. Sometimes when they were all sitting together she longed to ask, to find out, to get some public acknowledgment of the magic that lay over everything. At times it seemed as if could they all be still for a momentвҒ вҖ”it must take shape. It was everywhere, in the food, in the fragrance rising from the opened lid of the tea urn, in all the needful unquestioned movements, the requests, the handings and thanks, the going from room to room, the partings and assemblings. It hung about the fabrics and fittings of the house. Overwhelmingly it came in through oblongs of window giving on to stairways. Going upstairs in the light pouring in from some uncurtained window, she would cease for a moment to breathe.

Whenever she found herself alone she began to sing, softly. When she was with others a head drooped or lifted, the movement of a hand, the light falling along the detail of a profile could fill her with happiness.

It made companionship a perpetual question. At rare moments there would come a tingling from head to foot, a faint buzzing at her lips and at the tip of each finger. At these moments she could raise her eyes calmly to those about her and drink in the fact of their presence, see them all with perfect distinctness, but without distinguishing one from the other. She wanted to say, вҖңIsnвҖҷt it extraordinary? Do you realise?вҖқ She felt that if only she could make her meaning clear all difficulties must vanish. Outside in the open, going forward to some goal through sunny mornings, gathering at inns, wading through the scented undergrowth of the woods, she would dream of the secure return to WaldstraГҹe, their own beleaguered place. She saw it opening out warm and familiar back and back to the strange beginning in the winter. They would be there again tonight, singing.

One morning she knew that there was going to be a change. The term was coming to an end. There was to be a going away. The girls were talking about вҖңNorderney.вҖқ

вҖңGoing to Norderney, Hendy?вҖқ Jimmie said suddenly.

вҖңAh!вҖқ she responded mysteriously. For the rest of that day she sat contracted and fearful.

вҖңYou shall write and enquire of your good parents what they would have you do. You shall tell them that the German pupils return all to their homes; that the English pupils go for a happy holiday to the sea.вҖқ

вҖңOh yes,вҖқ said Miriam conversationally, with trembling breath.

вҖңIt is of course evident that since you will have no duties to perform, I cannot support the expense of your travelling and your maintenance.вҖқ

вҖңOh no, of course not,вҖқ said Miriam, her hands pressed against her knee.

She sat shivering in the warm dim Saal shaded by the close sun blinds. It looked as she had seen it with her father for the first time and FrГӨulein sitting near seemed to be once more in the heavy panniered blue velvet dress.

She waited stiff and ugly till FrГӨulein, secure and summer clad, spoke softly again.

вҖңYou think, my child, you shall like the profession of a teacher?вҖқ

вҖңOh yes,вҖқ said Miriam, from the midst of a tingling flush.

вҖңI think you have many qualities that make the teacher.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ You are earnest and serious-minded.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Grave.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Sometimes perhaps overgrave for your years.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But you have a serious faultвҒ вҖ”which must be corrected if you wish to succeed in your calling.вҖқ

Miriam tried to pull her features into an easy enquiring seriousness. A darkness was threatening her. вҖңYou have a most unfortunate manner.вҖқ

Without relaxing, Miriam quivered. She felt the blood mount to her head.

вҖңYou must adopt a quite, quite different manner. Your influence is, I think, good, a good English influence in its most general effect. But it is too slightly so and of too much indirection. You must exert it yourself, in a manner more alive, you must make it your aim that you shall have a responsible influence, a direct personal influence. You have too much of chill and formality. It makes a stiffness that I am willing to believe you do not intend.вҖқ

Miriam felt a faint dizziness.

вҖңIf you should fail to become more genial, more simple and natural as to your bearing, you will neither make yourself understood nor will you be loved by your pupils.вҖқ

вҖңNoвҒ вҖ”вҖқ responded Miriam, assuming an air of puzzled and interested consideration of FrГӨuleinвҖҷs words. She was recovering. She must get to the end of the interview and get away and find the answer. Far away beneath her fear and indignation, FrГӨulein was answered. She must get away and say the answer to herself.

вҖңTo truly fulfil the most serious role of the teacher you must enter into the personality of each pupil and must sympathise with the struggles of each one upon the path on which our feet are set. Efforts to good kindliness and thought for others must be encouraged. The teacher shall be sunshine, human sunshine, encouraging all effort and all lovely things in the personality of the pupil.вҖқ

FrГӨulein rose and stood, tall. Then her half-tottering decorous footsteps began. Miriam had hardly listened to her last words. She felt tears of anger rising and tried to smile.

вҖңI shall say now no more. But when you shall hear from your good parents, we can further discuss our plans.вҖқ FrГӨulein was at the door.

FrГӨulein left the Saal by the small door and Miriam felt her way to the schoolroom. The girls were gathering there ready for a walk. Some were in the hall and FrГӨuleinвҖҷs voice was giving instructions: вҖңMachen Sie schnell, Miss Henderson,вҖқ she called.

FrГӨulein had never before called to her like that. It had always been as if she did not see her but assumed her ready to fall in with the general movements.

Now it was FrГӨulein calling to her as she might do to Gertrude or Solomon. There was no hurried whisper from Jimmie telling her to вҖңfly for her life.вҖқ

вҖңJa, FrГӨulein,вҖқ she cried gaily and blundered towards the basement stairs. Mademoiselle was standing averted at the head of them; Miriam glanced at her. Her face was red and swollen with crying.

The sight amazed Miriam. She considered the swollen suffusion under the large black hat as she ran downstairs. She hoped Mademoiselle did not see her glance.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Mademoiselle, standing there all disfigured and blotchy about somethingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ it was nothingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ it couldnвҖҷt be anything.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ If anyone were dead she would not be standing thereвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ it was just some silly prim French quirkвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ her dignityвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ someone had been вҖңgrossiГЁreвҖқвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ and there she stood in her black hat and black cotton gloves.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Hurriedly putting on her hat and long lace scarf she decided that she would not change her shoes. Somewhere out in the sunshine a hurdy-gurdy piped out the air of вҖңDass du mich liebst, das wusstвҖҷ ich.вҖқ She glanced at the frosted barred window through which the dim light came into the dressing room. The piping notes, out of tune, wrongly emphasised, slurring one into the other, followed her across the dark basement hall and came faintly to her as she went slowly upstairs. There was no hurry. Everyone was talking busily in the hall, drowning the sound of her footsteps. She had forgotten her gloves. She went back into the cool grey musty rooms. A little crack in an upper pane shone like a gold thread. The barrel organ piped. As she stooped to gather up her gloves from the floor she felt the cold stone firm and secure under her hand. And the house stood up all round her with its rooms and the light lying along stairways and passages, and outside the bright hot sunshine and the roadways leading in all directions, out into Germany.

How could FrГӨulein possibly think she could afford to go to Norderney? They would all go. Things would go on. She could not go thereвҒ вҖ”nor back to England. It was cruelвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ just torture and worry againвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ with the bright house all round herвҒ вҖ”the high rooms, the dark old pianos, strange old garret, the unopened door beyond it. No help anywhere.

As they walked she laughed and talked with the girls, responding excitedly to all that was said. They walked along a broad and almost empty boulevard in two rows of four and five abreast, with Mademoiselle and Judy bringing up the rear. The talk was general and there was much laughter. It was the kind of interchange that arose when they were all together and there was anything вҖңin the air,вҖқ the kind that Miriam most disliked. She joined in it feverishly. ItвҖҷs perfectly natural that they should all be excited about the holidays she told herself, stifling her thoughts. But it must not go too far. They wanted to be jolly.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ If I could be jolly too they would like me. I must not be a wet blanket.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ MademoiselleвҖҷs voice was not heard. Miriam felt that the steering of the conversation might fall to anyone. Mademoiselle was extinguished. She must exert her influence. Presently she forgot MademoiselleвҖҷs presence altogether. They were all walking along very quickly.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ If she were going to Norderney with the English girls she must be on easy terms with them.

вҖңAh, ha!вҖқ somebody was saying.

вҖңOhвҖ‘ho!вҖқ said Miriam in response.

вҖңIhвҖ‘hi!вҖқ came another voice.

вҖңTreвҖ‘laвҖ‘la,вҖқ trilled Bertha Martin gently.

вҖңYou mean TurrahвҖ‘laheeвҖ‘tee,вҖқ said Miriam.

вҖңGood for you, Hendy,вҖқ blared Gertrude, in a swinging middle tone.

вҖңChalk it up. Chalk it up, children,вҖқ giggled Jimmie.

Millie looked pensively about her with vague disapproval. Her eyebrows were up. It seemed as if anything might happen; as if at any moment they might all begin running in different directions.

вҖңCave, my dear brats, be artig,вҖқ came BerthaвҖҷs cool even tones.

вҖңAh! we are observed.вҖқ

вҖңNo, we are not observed. The observer observeth not.вҖқ

Miriam saw her companions looking across the boulevard.

Following their eyes she found the figure of Pastor Lahmann walking swiftly bag in hand in the direction of an opening into a side street.

вҖңAh!вҖқ she cried gaily. вҖңVoilГ  Monsieur; courrez, Mademoiselle!вҖқ

At once she felt that it was cruel to draw attention to Mademoiselle when she was dumpy and upset.

вҖңWhat a fool I am,вҖқ she moaned in her mind. вҖңWhy canвҖҷt I say the right thing?вҖқ

вҖңCe nвҖҷest pas moi,вҖқ said Mademoiselle, вҖңqui fait les avances.вҖқ

The group walked on for a moment or two in silence. Bertha Martin was swinging her left foot out across the curb with each step, giving her right heel a little twirl to keep her balance.

вҖңYou are very clever BairвҖ‘ta,вҖқ said Mademoiselle, still in French, вҖңbut you will never make a prima ballerina.вҖқ

вҖңHulloh!вҖқ breathed Jimmie, вҖңsheвҖҷs perking up.вҖқ

вҖңIsnвҖҷt she,вҖқ said Miriam, feeling that she was throwing away the last shred of her dignity.

вҖңWhat was the matter?вҖқ she continued, trying to escape from her confusion.

MademoiselleвҖҷs instant response to her cry at the sight of Pastor Lahmann rang in her ears. She blushed to the soles of her feet.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ How could Mademoiselle misunderstand her insane remark? What did she mean? What did she really think of her? Just kind old LahmannвҒ вҖ”walking along there in the outside world.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ She did not want to stop him.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ He was a sort of kinsman for MademoiselleвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ that was what she had meant. Oh, why couldnвҖҷt she get away from all these girls?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ indeedвҒ вҖ”and again she saw the hurrying figure which had disappeared leaving the boulevard with its usual effect of a great strange oceanвҒ вҖ”he could have brought help and comfort to all of them if he had seen them and stopped. Pastor LahmannвҒ вҖ”LahmannвҒ вҖ”perhaps she would not see him again. Perhaps he could tell her what she ought to do.

вҖңOh, my dear,вҖқ Jimmie was saying, вҖңdidnвҖҷt you know?вҒ вҖ”a fearful row.вҖқ

MademoiselleвҖҷs laughter tinkled out from the rear.

вҖңA row?вҖқ

вҖңFearful!вҖқ JimmieвҖҷs face came round, round-eyed under her white sailor hat that sat slightly tilted on the peak of her hair.

вҖңWhat about?вҖқ

вҖңSomething about a letter or something, or some letters or somethingвҒ вҖ”I donвҖҷt know. Something she took out of the letter box, it was unlocked or something and Ulrica saw her and told Lily!вҖқ

вҖңGoodness!вҖқ breathed Miriam.

вҖңYes, and Lily had her in her room and Ulrica and poor little Petite couldnвҖҷt deny it. Ulrica said she did nothing but cry and cry. SheвҖҷs been crying all the morning, poor little pig.вҖқ

вҖңWhy did she want to take anything out of the box?вҖқ

вҖңOh, I donвҖҷt know. There was a fearful row anyhow. Ulrica said Lily talked like a clergymanвҒ вҖ”wie ein Pfarrer.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I donвҖҷt know. Ulrica said she was opening a letter. I donвҖҷt know.вҖқ

вҖңBut she canвҖҷt read German or English.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ

вҖңI donвҖҷt know. Ask me another.вҖқ

вҖңIt is extraordinary.вҖқ

вҖңWhatвҖҷs extraordinary?вҖқ asked Bertha from the far side of Jimmie.

вҖңPetite and that letter.вҖқ

вҖңOh.вҖқ

вҖңWhat did the Kiddy want?вҖқ

вҖңOh, my dear, donвҖҷt ask me to explain the peculiarities of the French temperament.вҖқ

вҖңYes, but all the letters in the letter box would be English or German, as Hendy says.вҖқ

Bertha glanced at Miriam. Miriam flushed. She could not discuss Mademoiselle with two of the girls at once.

вҖңRum go,вҖқ said Bertha.

вҖңYouвҖҷre right, my son. ItвҖҷs rum. ItвҖҷs all over now, anyhow. ThereвҖҷs no accounting for tastes. Poor old Petite.вҖқ

Miriam woke in the moonlight. She saw MademoiselleвҖҷs face as it had looked at teatime, pale and cruel, silent and very old. Someone had said she had been in FrГӨuleinвҖҷs room again all the afternoon.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ FrГӨulein had spoken to her once or twice during tea. She had answered coolly and eagerlyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ disgustingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ like a child that had been whipped and forgiven.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ How could FrГӨulein dare to forgive anybody?

She lay motionless. The night was cool. The screens had not been moved. She felt that the door was shut. After a while she began in imagination a conversation with Eve.

вҖңYou see the trouble was,вҖқ she said and saw EveвҖҷs downcast believing admiring sympathetic face, вҖңFrГӨulein talked to me about manner, she simply wanted me to grimace, simply. You knowвҒ вҖ”be like other people.вҖқ

Eve laughed. вҖңYes, I know.вҖқ

вҖңYou see? Simply.вҖқ

вҖңWell, if you wanted to stay, why couldnвҖҷt you?вҖқ

вҖңI simply couldnвҖҷt; you know how people are.вҖқ

вҖңBut you can act so splendidly.вҖқ

вҖңBut you canвҖҷt keep it up.вҖқ

вҖңWhy not?вҖқ

вҖңEve. There you are, you see, you always go back.вҖқ

вҖңI mean I think it would be simply lovely. If I were clever like you I should do it all the time, be simply always gushing and вҖҳcharming.вҖҷвҖҠвҖқ

Then she reminded Eve of the day they had walked up the lane to the Heath talking over all the manners they would like to haveвҒ вҖ”and how Sarah suddenly in the middle of supper had caricatured the one they had chosen. вҖңOf course you overdid it,вҖқ she concluded, and Eve crimsoned and said, вҖңOh yes, I know it was my fault. But you could have begun all over again in Germany and been quite different.вҖқ

вҖңYes, I know I thought about that.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ But if you knew as much of the world as I do.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ

Eve stared, showing a faint resentment.

Miriam thought of EveвҖҷs many suitors, of her six monthsвҖҷ betrothal, of her lifelong peacemaking, her experiment in being governess to the two children of an artistвҒ вҖ”a little green-robed boy threatening her with a knife.

вҖңYes, but I mean if you had been about.вҖқ

вҖңI know,вҖқ smiled Eve confidently. вҖңYou mean if I were you. Go on. I know. Explain, old thing.вҖқ

вҖңWell, I mean of course if you are a governess in a school you canвҖҷt be jolly and charming. You canвҖҷt be idiotic or anything.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ I did think about it. DonвҖҷt tell anybody. But I thought for a little while I might go into a familyвҒ вҖ”one of the girlsвҖҷ familiesвҒ вҖ”the German girls, and begin having a German manner. Two of the girls asked me. One of them was ill and went awayвҒ вҖ”that Pomeranian one I told you about. Well, then, I didnвҖҷt tell you about that little one and her sisterвҒ вҖ”they asked me to go to them for the holidays. The youngest saidвҒ вҖ”it was so absurdвҒ вҖ”вҖҳyou shall marry my bruzzerвҒ вҖ”he is mairchantвҒ вҖ”very weltyвҖҷвҒ вҖ”absurd.вҖқ

вҖңNot absurdвҒ вҖ”you probably would have, away from that school.вҖқ

вҖңDвҖҷyou think so?вҖқ

вҖңYes, you would have been a regular German, fat and jolly and laughing.вҖқ

вҖңI know. My dear I thought about it. You may imagine. I wondered if I ought.вҖқ

вҖңWhy didnвҖҷt you try?вҖқ

Why not? Why was she not going to try? Eve would, she was sure in her place.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ

Why not grimace and be very вҖңbrightвҖқ and вҖңanimatedвҖқ until the end of the term and then go and stay with the Bergmanns for two months and be as charming as she could?вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Her heart sank.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ She imagined a house, everyone kind and blond and smiling. EmmaвҖҷs big tall brother smiling and joking and liking her. She would laugh and pretend and flirt like the Pooles and make up to himвҒ вҖ”and it would be lovely for a little while. Then she would offend someone. She would offend everyone but EmmaвҒ вҖ”and get tired and cross and lose her temper. Stare at them all as they said the things everybody said, the things she hated; and she would sit glowering, and suddenly refuse to allow the women to be familiar with her.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ She tried to see the brother more clearly. She looked at the screen. The BergmannsвҖҷ house would be full of German furniture.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ At the end of a week every bit of it would reproach her.

She tried to imagine him without the house and the family, not talking or joking or pretendingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ alone and sadвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ despising his familyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ needing her. He loved forests and music. He had a great strong solid voice and was strong and sure about everything and she need never worry any more.

вҖңSeit ich ihn gesehen
GlaubвҖҷ ich blind zu sein.вҖқ

There would be a garden and German springs and summers and sunsets and strong kind arms and a shoulder. She would grow so happy. No one would recognise her as the same person. She would wear a band of turquoise-blue velvet ribbon round her hair and look at the mountains.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ No good. She could never get out to that. Never. She could not pretend long enough. Everything would be at an end long before there was any chance of her turning into a happy German woman.

Certainly with a German man she would be angry at once. She thought of the men she had seenвҒ вҖ”in the streets, in cafГ©s and gardens, the masters in the school, photographs in the girlsвҖҷ albums. They had all offended her at once. Something in their bearing and manner.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Blind and impudent.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ She thought of the interview she had witnessed between Ulrica and her cousinвҒ вҖ”the cousin coming up from the estate in Erfurth, arriving in a carriage, FrГӨuleinвҖҷs manner, her smiles and hints; Ulrica standing in the Saal in her sprigged saffron muslin dress curtseyingвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ with bent head, the cousinвҖҷs condescending laughing voice. It would never do for her to go into a German home. She must not say anything about the chance of going to the BergmannsвҖҷвҒ вҖ”even to Eve.

She imagined Eve sitting listening in the window space in the bow that was carpeted with linoleum to look like parquet flooring. Beyond them lay the length of the Turkey carpet darkening away under the long table. She could see each object on the shining sideboard. The silver biscuit box and the large Г©pergne made her feel guilty and shifting, guilty from the beginning of things.

вҖңYou see, Eve, I thought counting it all up that if I came home it would cost less than going to Norderney and that all the expense of my going to Germany and coming back is less than what it would have cost to keep me at home for the five months IвҖҷve been thereвҒ вҖ”I wish youвҖҷd tell everybody that.вҖқ

She turned about in bed; her head was growing fevered.

She conjured up a vision of the backs of the books in the bookcase in the dining room at home.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Iliad and OdysseyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ people going over the sea in boats and someone doing embroideryвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ that little picture of Hector and Andromache in the corner of a pageвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ he in armourвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ she, in a trailing dress, holding up her baby. Both, silly.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ She wished she had read more carefully. She could not remember anything in Lecky or Darwin that would tell her what to doвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ HudibrasвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ The Atomic TheoryвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Ballads and Poems, D. G. RossettiвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ KinglakeвҖҷs CrimeaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ PalgraveвҖҷs ArabiaвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Crimea.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ The Crimea.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Florence Nightingale; a picture somewhere; a refined face, with cap and strings.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ She must have smiled.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ MotleyвҖҷs Rise ofвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Rise ofвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ MotleyвҖҷs Rise of the Dutch Republic.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ MotleyвҖҷs Rise of the Dutch Republic and the Chronicles of the SchГ¶nberg-Cotta Family. She held to the memory of these two books. Something was coming from them to her. She handled the shiny brown gold-tooled back of MotleyвҖҷs Rise and felt the hard graining of the red-bound Chronicles.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ There were green trees outside in the moonlightвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ in LutherвҖҷs GermanyвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ trees and fields and German towns and then Holland. She breathed more easily. Her eyes opened serenely. Tranquil moonlight lay across the room. It surprised her like a sudden hand stroking her brow. It seemed to feel for her heart. If she gave way to it her thoughts would go. Perhaps she ought to watch it and let her thoughts go. It passed over her trouble like her mother did when she said, вҖңDonвҖҷt go so deeply into everything, chickie. You must learn to take life as it comes. AhвҖ‘eh if I were strong I could show you how to enjoy life.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰвҖқ Delicate little mother, running quickly downstairs clearing her throat to sing. But mother did not know. She had no reasoning power. She could not help because she did not know. The moonlight was sad and hesitating. Miriam closed her eyes again. LutherвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ pinning up that notice on a church door.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ (Why is Luther like a dyspeptic blackbird? вҖңEhвҖ‘h,вҖқ she sneered. вҖңI know, I know who are the culprits. I have always known.вҖқ She gasped. вҖңIt shall ceaseвҒ вҖ”these talksвҒ вҖ”this vile talk of men. Do you understand? It shall cease. IвҒ вҖ”willвҒ вҖ”notвҒ вҖ”haveвҒ вҖ”it.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ The school shall be cleanвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ from pupil to pupilвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ from room to room.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Every dayвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ every hour.вҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ Shameless!вҖқ she screamed. вҖңShameless. Ah! I know. I know you.вҖқ She stood with her arms folded, swaying, and gave a little laugh. вҖңYou think to deceive me. You do not deceive me. I know. I have known and I shall know. This school is mine. Mine! My place! I will have it as I will have it. That is clear and plain, and you all shall help me. I shall say no more. But I shall know what to do.вҖқ

Mechanically Miriam went downstairs with the rest of the party. With the full force of her nerves she resisted the echoes of FrГӨuleinвҖҷs onslaught, refusing to think of anything she had said and blotting out her image every time it rose. The essential was that she would be dismissed as Mademoiselle had been dismissed. That was the upshot of it all for her. FrГӨulein was a mad, silly, pious female who would send her away and go on glowering over the Bible. She would have to go, go, go in a sort of disgrace.

The girls were talking all round her, excitedly. She despised them for showing that they were disturbed by FrГӨuleinвҖҷs despotic nonsense. As they reached the basement she remembered the letter crushed in her hand and sat down on the last step to glance through it.

вҖңDearest Mim. I have a wonderful piece of news for you. I wonder what you will say? It is about Harriett. She has asked me to tell you as she does not like to write about it herself.вҖқ

With steady hands Miriam turned the closely-written sheets reading a phrase here and thereвҒ вҖҠвҒ вҖҰ вҖңregularly in the seat behind us at All SaintsвҖҷ for monthsвҒ вҖ”saw her with the Pooles at a concert at the Assembly Rooms and made up his mind thenвҒ вҖ”the moment he saw herвҒ вҖ”joined the tennis clubвҒ вҖ”they won the double handicapвҒ вҖ”a beautiful Slazenger racquetвҒ вҖ”only just over sixteenвҒ вҖ”for yearsвҒ вҖ”of course Mother says itвҖҷs just a little foolish nonsenseвҒ вҖ”but I am not sure that she really thinks soвҒ вҖ”Gerald took me into his confidenceвҒ вҖ”made a solemn callвҒ вҖ”admirably suited to each otherвҒ вҖ”rather a long melancholy good-looking faceвҒ вҖ”they look such a contrastвҒ вҖ”the big Canadian RailwayвҒ вҖ”not exactly a clerkвҒ вҖ”something rather above that, to do with making drafts of things and so on. Very sweet and charmingвҒ вҖ”my own young daysвҒ вҖ”that I have reached the great age of twenty-threeвҒ вҖ”resident post in the countryвҒ вҖ”two little girlsвҒ вҖ”we think it very good payвҒ вҖ”I shall go in SeptemberвҒ вҖ”plenty of timeвҒ вҖ”that you should come home for the long holidays. We are all looking forward to itвҒ вҖ”the tennis clubвҒ вҖ”your name as a holiday memberвҒ вҖ”the American tournament in AugustвҒ вҖ”Harry was the youngest lady member like youвҒ вҖ”of course Harry could not let you come without knowingвҒ вҖ”find somebody travelling throughвҒ вҖ”FrГӨulein PfaffвҒ вҖ”expect to see you looking like a flour sack with a string tied round its waistвҒ вҖ”all the dwarf roses in bloomвҒ вҖ”hardly any strawberriesвҒ вҖ”we shall see you soonвҒ вҖ”everybody sends.вҖқ

Miriam got up and swung the half-read letter above her head like a dumbbell.

She looked about her like a strangerвҒ вҖ”everything was as it had been the day she cameвҒ вҖ”the little cramped basement hallвҒ вҖ”the strange German girlsвҒ вҖ”small and old looking, poking about amongst the baskets. She hardly knew them. She passed half-blindly amongst them with her eyes wide. The little dressing room seemed full of bright light. She saw everyone at once clearly. All the English girls were there. She knew every line of each of them. They were her old friends. They knew her. Looking at none of them she felt she embraced them all, closely, and that they knew it. They shone. They were beautiful. She wanted to cry aloud. She was English and free. She had nothing to do with this German school. Baskets at her feet made her pick her way. Solomon was kneeling at one, sorting and handing out. At a little table under the window Millie stood jotting pencil notes in a pocketbook. Judy was at her side. The others were grouped about the piano. Gertrude sat on the keyboard her legs dangling.

Miriam plumped down on a full basket.

вҖңHullo, Hendy, old chap, you look all right!вҖқ

Miriam looked fearlessly up at the faces that were turned towards her. Again she seemed to see all of them at once. The circle of her vision seemed huge. It was as if the confining rim of her glasses were gone and she saw equally from eyes that seemed to fill her face. She drew all their eyes to her. They were waiting for her to speak. For a moment it seemed as if they stood there lifeless. She had drawn all their meaning and all their happiness into herself. She could do as she wished with themвҒ вҖ”their poor little lives.

They stood waiting for some word from her. She dropped her eyes and caught the flash of GertrudeвҖҷs swinging steel buckles.

вҖңWasnвҖҷt FrГӨulein angry?вҖқ she said carelessly.

Someone pushed the door to.

вҖңSly old bird.вҖқ

вҖңFancy imagining we shouldnвҖҷt see through Mademoiselle leaving.вҖқ

вҖңHвҖҷm,вҖқ said Miriam.

вҖңI knew Mademoiselle would sneak if she had half a chance.вҖқ

вҖңYes, ever since she got so thick with Elsa.вҖқ

вҖңOh!вҒ вҖ”Elsa.вҖқ

вҖңYou bet FrГӨulein looks down on the two of them in her heart of hearts.вҖқ

вҖңMвҖҷmвҒ вҖ”sheвҖҷs fairly sick, Jemima, with the lot of us this time.вҖқ

вҖңMademoiselle told her some pretty things,вҖқ laughed Gertrude. вҖңLily thinks weвҖҷre lost soulsвҒ вҖ”nearly all of us.вҖқ

вҖңOnny swaw, my dears, onny swaw.вҖқ

вҖңItвҖҷs all very well. But thereвҖҷs no knowing what Mademoiselle would make her believe. SheвҖҷd got reams about you, HendyвҒ вҖ”nothing bad enough.вҖқ

вҖңHвҖҷm,вҖқ said Miriam, вҖңI can imagineвҒ вҖ”вҖқ

Her thoughts brought back a day when she had shown Mademoiselle the names in her birthday book and dwelt on one page and let Mademoiselle understand that it was the pageвҒ вҖ”brown eyesвҒ вҖ”les yeux brunes foncГ©s. Why did Mademoiselle and FrГӨulein think that badвҒ вҖ”want to spoil it for her? She had said nothing about the confidences of the German girls to anyone. Elsa must have found that out from Clara.

вҖңOh, well itвҖҷs all over now. LetвҖҷs be thankful and think no more about it.вҖқ

вҖңAll very fine, Jemima. YouвҖҷre going home.вҖқ

вҖңThank goodness.вҖқ

вҖңAnd not coming back. Lucky Pigleinchen.вҖқ

вҖңWell, so am I,вҖқ said Miriam, вҖңand IвҖҷm not coming back.вҖқ

вҖңI say! ArenвҖҷt you coming to Norderney?вҖқ Gertrude flashed dark eyes at her.

вҖңCanвҖҷt you come to Norderney?вҖқ said Judy thickly, at her elbow.

вҖңWell, you see there are all sorts of things happening at home. I must go. One of my sisters is engaged and another going away. I must go home for a while. Of course I might come back.вҖқ

вҖңThink it over, Henderson, and see if you canвҖҷt decide in our favour.вҖқ

вҖңWe shall have another Miss Owen.вҖқ

Miriam struggled up out of her basket. вҖңBut I thought you all liked Miss Owen!вҖқ

вҖңHo! Goodness! Too simple for words.вҖқ

вҖңYou never told us you had any sisters, Hendy,вҖқ said Jimmie, tapping her on the wrist.

вҖңWhat a pity youвҖҷre going just as weвҖҷre getting to know you,вҖқ Judy smiled shyly and looked on the floor.

вҖңWellвҒ вҖ”IвҖҷm off with my bundle,вҖқ announced Gertrude. вҖңTo be continued in our next. Think it over, Hendy. DonвҖҷt desert us. Hurry up, my room. ItвҖҷll be teatime before weвҖҷre straight. Come on, Jim.вҖқ

Miriam moved, with Judy following at her elbow, across the room to Millie. She looked up with her little plaintive frown. Miriam could not remember what her plans were. вҖңLetвҖҷs see,вҖқ she said, вҖңyouвҖҷre going to Norderney, arenвҖҷt you?вҖқ

вҖңIвҖҷm not going to Norderney,вҖқ said Millie almost tearfully. вҖңI only wish I were. I donвҖҷt even know IвҖҷm coming back next term.вҖқ

вҖңArenвҖҷt you looking forward to the holidays?вҖқ

вҖңI donвҖҷt know. IвҖҷd rather be staying here if IвҖҷm not coming back after.вҖқ

вҖңTo stay in Germany? YouвҖҷd rather do that than anything?вҖқ

вҖңRather.вҖқ

вҖңHere, with FrГӨulein Pfaff?вҖқ

вҖңOf course, here with FrГӨulein Pfaff. IвҖҷd rather be in Germany than anything.вҖқ

Millie stood staring with her pout and her slightly raised eyebrows at the frosted window.

вҖңWould you stay here in the school for the holidays if FrГӨulein were staying?вҖқ

вҖңIвҖҷd do anything,вҖқ said Millie, вҖңto stay in Germany.вҖқ

вҖңYou know,вҖқ said Miriam gazing at her, вҖңso would IвҒ вҖ”any mortal thing.вҖқ

MillieвҖҷs eyes had filled with tears.

вҖңThen why donвҖҷt ye stay?вҖқ said Judy, with gentle gruffness.

The house was shut up for the night.

Miriam looked up at the clock dizzily as she drank the last of her coffee. It marked half past eleven. FrГӨulein had told her to be ready at a quarter to twelve. Her hands felt large and shaky and her feet were cold. The room was stiflingвҒ вҖ”bare and brown in the gaslight. She left it and crept through the hall where her trunk stood and up the creaking stairs. She turned up the gas. Emma lay asleep with red eyelids and cheeks. Miriam did not look at Ulrica. Hurriedly and desolately she packed her bag. She was going home empty-handed. She had achieved nothing. FrГӨulein had made not the slightest effort to keep her. She was just nothing againвҒ вҖ”with her Saratoga trunk and her handbag. Harriett had achieved. Harriett. She was just going home with nothing to say for herself.

вҖңThe carriage is here, my child. Make haste.вҖқ

Miriam pushed things hurriedly into her bag. FrГӨulein had gone downstairs.

She was ready. She looked numbly round the room. Emma looked very far away. She turned out the gas. The dim light from the landing shone into the room. She stood for a moment in the doorway looking back. The room seemed to be empty. There seemed to be nothing in it but the black screen standing round the bed that was no longer hers.

вҖңGoodbye,вҖқ she murmured and hurried downstairs.

In the hall FrГӨulein began to talk at once, talking until they were seated side by side in the dark cab.

Then Miriam gazed freely at the pale profile shining at her side. Poor FrГӨulein Pfaff, getting old.

FrГӨulein began to ask about MiriamвҖҷs plans for the future. Miriam answered as to an equal, elaborating a little account of circumstances at home, and the doings of her sisters. As she spoke she felt that FrГӨulein envied her her youth and her family at home in EnglandвҒ вҖ”and she raised her voice a little and laughed easily and moved, crossing her knees in the cab.

She used sentimental German words about HarriettвҒ вҖ”a description of her that might have applied to EmmaвҒ вҖ”little emphatic tender epithets came to her from the conversations of the girls. FrГӨulein praised her German warmly and asked question after question about the house and garden at Barnes and presently of her mother.

вҖңI canвҖҷt talk about her,вҖқ said Miriam shortly.

вҖңThat is English,вҖқ murmured FrГӨulein.

вҖңSheвҖҷs such a little thing,вҖқ said Miriam, вҖңsmaller than any of us.вҖқ Presently FrГӨulein laid her gloved hand on MiriamвҖҷs gloved one. вҖңYou and I have, I think, much in common.вҖқ

Miriam frozeвҒ вҖ”and looked at the gas lamps slowly swinging by along the boulevard. вҖңMuch will have happened in England whilst you have been here with us,вҖқ said FrГӨulein eagerly.

They reached a streetвҒ вҖ”shuttered darkness where the shops were, and here and there the yellow flare of a cafГ©. She strained her eyes to see the faces and forms of men and womenвҒ вҖ”breathing more quickly as she watched the characteristic German gait.

There was the station.

Her trunk was weighed and registered. There was something to pay. She handed her purse to FrГӨulein and stood gazing at the uniformed manвҒ вҖ”ruddy and clear-eyedвҒ вҖ”clear hard blue eyes and hard clean clear yellow moustachesвҒ вҖ”decisive untroubled movements. Passengers were walking briskly about and laughing and shouting remarks to each other. The train stood waiting for her. The ringing of an enormous bell brought her hands to her ears. FrГӨulein gently propelled her up the three steps into a compartment marked Damen-CoupГ©. It smelt of biscuits and wine.

A man with a booming voice came to examine her ticket. He stood bending under the central light, uttering sturdy German words. Miriam drank them in without understanding. He left the carriage very empty. The great bell was ringing again. FrГӨulein standing on the top step pressed both her hands and murmured words of farewell.

вҖңLebвҖҷ wohl, mein Kind, Gott segne dich.вҖқ

вҖңGoodbye, FrГӨulein,вҖқ she said stiffly, shaking hands.

The door was shut with a slamвҒ вҖ”the light seemed to go down. Miriam glanced at itвҒ вҖ”half the dull green muslin shade had slipped over the gas globe. The carriage seemed dark. The platform outside was very bright. FrГӨulein had disappeared. The train was high above the platform. Politely smiling Miriam scrambled to the window. The platform was moving, the large bright station moving away. FrГӨuleinвҖҷs wide smile was creasing and caverning under her hat from which the veil was thrown back.

Standing at the window Miriam smiled sharply. FrГӨuleinвҖҷs form flowed slowly away with the platform.

Groups passed by smiling and waving.

Miriam sat down.

She leaped up to lean from the window.

The platform had disappeared.
